search_results.title: ./param-camel-escaped.txt
search_results.snippet:
userName = marcell								// User Name that will be used in the file name.
cycleLength = 2000.0				// Time period of one cycle (msec).
cellModel = ToR-ORd-dynCl_epi // Name of the cell model. Refer to the guide to fill it
stimulusDuration = 0.5							// Duration of stimulus cur...

message.content:
```latex
\section*{Methods}

Simulations were performed using the Comprehensive in vitro Proarrhythmia Assay (CiPA) initiative framework, an internationally standardized protocol for assessing proarrhythmic risk through in silico models of the human ventricular action potential. The ToR-ORd-dynCl model of human ventricular cardiomyocyte with dynamic intracellular chloride (epicardial variant) was employed, incorporating multi-channel ionic current inhibition data derived from drug concentration-response experiments for quinidine at concentrations of 3237 nM, 6474 nM, 9711 nM, and 12948 nM.[13] These models predict Torsade de Pointes (TdP) liability by quantifying electrophysiological biomarkers such as action potential duration and net charge movement, with pacing applied at a cycle length of 2000 ms, stimulus duration of 0.5 ms, and amplitude scale of 1.0 using the CVode solver with time steps between 0.5 ms and 2.0 ms.[13]

Key steps in the simulation workflow included:
\begin{itemize}
\item Loading IC50 samples from \nolinkurl{./chantest-hill/quinidine/IC50_samples30.csv} and hERG parameters from \nolinkurl{./chantest-herg/quinidine/boot_pars10.csv} to parameterize multi-channel blockade via Hill equations.[13]
\item Initializing states from \nolinkurl{./initial-values-zips/quinidine-ToR-ORd-dynCl_epi_initial-values.zip} and running parallel simulations across 10 CPUs.[13]
\item Computing time-series traces for membrane voltage, ionic currents, and intracellular calcium over steady-state cycles.[12]
\item Extracting biomarkers including APD90, APD50, maximum upstroke velocity, and depolarization failure rates.[12][13]
\end{itemize}

A primary quantitative endpoint was qNet, defined as the net electronic charge carried by the major ionic currents during one cardiac action potential:
\[
q_{\text{Net}} = \int_{0}^{T} \left( I_{\text{Na}} + I_{\text{CaL}} + I_{\text{Kr}} + I_{\text{Ks}} + I_{\text{to}} + I_{\text{K1}} \right) \, dt
\]
where \(T\) is the duration of one cardiac cycle, and \(I_{\text{Na}}\), \(I_{\text{CaL}}\), \(I_{\text{Kr}}\), \(I_{\text{Ks}}\), \(I_{\text{to}}\), and \(I_{\text{K1}}\) represent the sodium, L-type calcium, rapid and slow delayed-rectifier potassium, transient outward potassium, and inward rectifier potassium currents, respectively. Lower qNet values tend to associate with reduced repolarization reserve and higher proarrhythmic potential, as endorsed by CiPA modeling working groups. All simulations and qNet computations were performed using rigorously validated numerical solvers and verified parameter consistency under CiPA reference conditions.[12][13]
```
